docs.title: Welcome to TrySpace Atlas
docs.text: The TrySpace Atlas is your comprehensive guide to understanding and working with TrySpace Lab. This documentation is designed to support users, developers, and contributors by providing clear and detailed information.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text:
• User Guides: Step-by-step instructions for setting up and using TrySpace Lab.
  • Examples and Tutorials: Practical examples and walkthroughs to help users get started quickly.
  • Developer Documentation: Detailed explanations of the architecture, codebase, and APIs.

docs.title: First Steps
docs.text: To begin, explore the sections above or use the search functionality to find specific topics. If you have questions or need help, feel free to start a GitHub Discussion. For bug reports or feature requests, please open a GitHub Issue. We welcome contributions from everyone. Please review our Contributing Guidelines in the tryspace-lab repository for details on how to get started.

docs.title: About TrySpace
docs.text: TrySpace is a space software and systems design company founded to accelerate space mission development through open-source tools, software digital twins, and a mission-first approach.

docs.title: Mission & Philosophy
docs.text: To democratize access to high-quality space system development tools through pragmatic, open-source, software-first infrastructure that scales from CubeSats to interplanetary missions.

docs.title: Founder Background
docs.text:
TrySpace was founded by a NASA software developer with over a decade of experience in embedded systems, flight software, and simulation infrastructure.

Key contributions include CryptoLib and the NASA Operational Simulator for Small Satellites (NOS3) — tools trusted across multiple NASA missions for rapid prototyping, testing, and operations.

docs.title: What We Do
docs.text:
• Software-only digital twins for space systems
  • End-to-end mission simulation and architecture
  • Custom tools for flight and ground systems
  • Integration and support for open-source space software
  • Technical consulting

docs.title: Why TrySpace?
docs.text: Building for space is hard. We provide the software solutions that make it easier to try, test, and fly. TrySpace lowers the barriers to entry, increases reuse, and enables your mission to launch smarter.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text:
We'd love to hear from you. Whether you’re starting a new mission, looking for support with an existing one, or just want to connect — get in touch.

  • Email: John.P.Lucas@tryspace.org
  • GitHub: https://github.com/TrySpaceOrg
  • Location: Morgantown, WV

Please include a short description of your project, timeframe, and what support you need when you reach out.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: This page documents the orchestrator and configuration files used by TrySpace Lab.

docs.title: Primary config files (in cfg/)
docs.text:
• tryspace-config.yaml - global configuration that lists available missions and global defaults.
  • Mission config files - referenced from tryspace-config.yaml (each mission entry contains a config_file and scenarios).
  • Scenario config files - referenced from a mission config and provide scenario-specific settings and optional overrides.
  • active.yaml - the current selection of mission and scenario (created automatically with defaults if missing).
  • build.yaml - a merged, human-readable snapshot written by the orchestrator for inspection.

docs.title: Orchestrator (cfg/tryspace-orchestrator.py)
docs.text:
• Loads active.yaml, tryspace-config.yaml, the selected mission, and the selected scenario.
  • If active.yaml does not exist, it will create one using the first mission and scenario found in tryspace-config.yaml / mission config.
  • Merges configuration layers and writes the merged result to cfg/build.yaml.
  • For each component listed under the mission's components key the orchestrator performs a cascading merge and attempts to render component templates.

docs.title: Merge/override precedence (applied in this order, later items win)
docs.text:
• Fallback component defaults: comp/<component>/support/device_config.yaml (if present)
  • Global config (tryspace-config.yaml) for the component
  • Mission-level config for the component
  • Scenario-level config for the component
  • Scenario-level overrides (flat dict applied last)

docs.title: Template rendering
docs.text:
• The orchestrator looks for a Jinja2 template at comp/<component>/support/device_config.j2
  • If a template and merged config exist, it renders the template with config and writes the output to comp/<component>/shared/device_cfg.h (creates directories as needed)

docs.title: Running the orchestrator
docs.text:
Run via make:

make cfg

Also can run directly:

python3 cfg/tryspace-orchestrator.py

docs.title: Changing the active mission/scenario
docs.text:
Edit cfg/active.yaml and set the desired mission and scenario, then run the orchestrator to regenerate cfg/build.yaml and component configs.

Example cfg/active.yaml:

mission: drm
scenario: nominal

docs.title: Notes & tips
docs.text:
• Inspect cfg/build.yaml after a run to verify the merged configuration
  • Component templates expect a flattened config in the config context — place component-specific keys under the component's top-level key in global/mission/scenario files

docs.title: Data and Cleanup
docs.text: Details about persistent data, named volumes, and safe cleanup.

docs.title: Named volumes used
docs.text:
• gsw-data - stores GSW/YAMCS persistent data (intended to survive restarts for post-processing).
  • simulith_ipc - used for inter-process IPC between simulith components.

List volumes:

docker volume ls

Inspect a volume's mountpoints:

docker volume inspect gsw-data

docs.title: Common cleanup commands
docs.text:
• Stop containers and keep volumes (safe default):
make stop
  • Clean built files, volumes, and networks:
make clean

docs.title: Safety notes
docs.text:
• make clean is destructive
  • Confirm backups or exports before running down --volumes on production or long-running datasets

docs.title: Getting Started with TrySpace Lab
docs.text: A short quick-start to get a running TrySpace Lab environment. For detailed reference see the linked pages (Installation, Configuration, Operations, Data & Cleanup, Troubleshooting).

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
• Ensure Docker, Docker Compose (v2+), Make and Git are installed (see installation.md).
  • Clone and initialize submodules:
git clone https://github.com/TrySpaceOrg/tryspace-lab.git
cd tryspace-lab
git submodule update --init --recursive
  • Prepare environment and build:
make
  • Start the lab:
make start

  • Access services (examples):

  • GSW (YAMCS): http://localhost:8090

  • Attach to consoles: docker attach tryspace-server

  • Stop (preserves data):

make stop

docs.text:

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This page lists prerequisites and OS-specific tips for installing TrySpace Lab dependencies.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Docker Engine
  • Docker Compose v2 (CLI plugin) or newer
  • Make
  • Git

docs.title: Windows notes
docs.text: • Prefer WSL2 and Docker Engine

docs.title: Verifying installation
docs.text:
docker --version
docker compose version
make --version
git --version

docs.title: Operations
docs.text: Standard operations and common commands for running TrySpace Lab.

docs.title: Starting the lab
docs.text:
Foreground (useful for development and observing operations):

make start

docs.title: Attaching and logs
docs.text:
Attach to a running container:

docker attach tryspace-server

Follow logs for all services:

docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs -f

Follow logs for a single service:

docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs -f tryspace-server

docs.title: tryspace-server
docs.text:
• Purpose: Core server process and controls the time synronization provided by Simulith across the environment
  • Typical use: pause/play or change simulation speed
  • Common commands:
docker attach tryspace-server
docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs -f tryspace-server

docs.title: tryspace-director
docs.text:
• Purpose: Loads component simulators and 42 libraries in time step with environment via Simulith
  • Typical use: inspect logs to verify component interfacing with flight software
  • Common commands:
docker attach tryspace-director
docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs -f tryspace-director

docs.title: tryspace-fsw
docs.text:
• Purpose: Flight Software (FSW) container that runs the core Flight System software with component applications.
  • Typical use: check FSW startup, telemetry output, and interactions
  • Common commands:
docker attach tryspace-fsw
docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs -f tryspace-fsw

docs.title: tryspace-gsw
docs.text:
• Purpose: Ground Software (GSW) container running YAMCS (ground station interface and telemetry archive).
  • Typical use: open the web UI, examine telemetry, and export or inspect persistent data.
  • Ports: 8090 -> 8090 (host) for the YAMCS web UI (confirm in cfg/lab-compose.yml).
  • Volumes:
  • gsw-data — persistent YAMCS data (preserved across docker compose down unless --volumes is used).
  • Healthcheck: Compose defines an HTTP healthcheck against http://localhost:8090 (see cfg/lab-compose.yml).
  • Common commands:
# Open UI in a browser
firefox http://localhost:8090

docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs -f tryspace-gsw
# inspect persistent data
docker volume inspect gsw-data

docs.title: Stopping
docs.text:
Stop containers and remove networks (preserves volumes):

make stop

Stop and remove containers, network, and named volumes (clean state):

make clean

docs.title: Uninstalling
docs.text:
To uninstall all pieces of the tryspace environment:

make uninstall

docs.title: Troubleshooting & FAQ
docs.text: Short troubleshooting checklist and common issues.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Are containers running? docker ps
  • Are logs showing errors? docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs --tail 200
  • Are ports free? ss -ltnp | grep 8090
  • Are volumes filling up? docker system df --volumes

docs.title: Common issues
docs.text:
• Data keeps growing across runs

  • Reason: Named Docker volumes persist. To reset, run docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml down --volumes.

  • Port conflicts (e.g., 8090)

  • Fix: Stop the conflicting service or modify cfg/lab-compose.yml to use a different host port.

  • Container crashes on startup

  • Inspect logs and raise an issue with the output if you need help. Useful commands:
# show recent logs for a service
docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs --tail 200 tryspace-server
# inspect container exit code
docker inspect --format='{{.State.ExitCode}}' tryspace-server

docs.title: When to open an issue
docs.text:
• Reproducible crashes with logs and steps to reproduce
  • Misbehavior in orchestrator merges (include cfg/build.yaml snapshot)
  • Unexpected data corruption in persistent volumes

docs.title: Reporting checklist for issues
docs.text:
• git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD (branch)
  • git rev-parse --short HEAD (commit)
  • docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml ps output
  • Relevant logs (attach docker compose -f ./cfg/lab-compose.yml logs --tail 500)
  • cfg/build.yaml (after running orchestrator)
  • cfg/active.yaml
